question: A server holds 10,000 mostly-idle WebSocket connections, one blocking thread each. Almost all of those threads are sitting inside recv() waiting for data. What is that costing the machine right now?
options: A great deal of CPU: each thread repeatedly checks whether its socket has data, so 10,000 threads means 10,000 poll loops competing for cores
Nothing measurable — a thread waiting on I/O is completely free until data arrives, which is exactly why thread-per-connection scales indefinitely

Almost no CPU but a large amount of memory and scheduling capacity: each parked thread is off the run queue consuming zero cycles, yet still holds 8.0 MiB of reserved stack (78.1 GiB across 10,000) and 16.3 KiB resident, and costs ~16.8 us of scheduler work every time it is woken
Mostly network bandwidth, because each blocked recv() keeps a TCP keep-alive probe in flight for its socket
explanation: A blocking recv() on an empty buffer does not spin — the kernel puts the thread on the socket's wait queue, marks it TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE and takes it off the run queue, so it executes no instructions at all until a packet's interrupt handler wakes it. That is why the bill is memory and scheduling rather than cycles, and why 'blocking I/O is slow' is the wrong diagnosis. Option A is the tempting one because it confuses blocking with polling; polling is what you get when you set O_NONBLOCK and retry in a loop.

question: You set sock.setblocking(False) and wrap recv() in a retry loop that catches BlockingIOError. Compared to just letting the blocking recv() park, what happens while you wait 250 ms for one message?
options: CPU time goes from 0.1 ms to 249.6 ms — 99.7% of a core saturated, a 3,081x increase — issuing 293,323 failed recv() syscalls at ~1.17 million per second, all of them returning 'nothing to do' and producing no result whatsoever
Latency improves substantially, because the retry loop notices the data microseconds after it lands instead of waiting for the scheduler
Nothing changes at the machine level; O_NONBLOCK only affects the error code returned, so both versions consume identical resources
Memory use rises sharply because each retry allocates a fresh receive buffer, but CPU use stays flat
explanation: O_NONBLOCK converts a free park into a paid spin: you have replaced 'the kernel will wake me' with 'I will ask, endlessly'. The latency argument in option B is technically true by a few microseconds and is precisely why people write this loop, but it costs an entire core per connection to buy it — completely unaffordable at any real connection count. Non-blocking mode is only useful once something else (select/poll/epoll/kqueue) tells you when to act.

question: With exactly one ready descriptor throughout, select() was measured at 1.1 us for 11 watched descriptors, 5.2 us for 101, 21.5 us for 501 and 41.5 us for 1,001, while epoll_wait() stayed at 0.9 us at every size. At 2,001 descriptors select() did not merely get slower. What happened, and why?
options: select() started dropping events silently, because the kernel's internal ready list overflowed and older entries were evicted
select() blocked forever, because a descriptor beyond the limit can never be marked readable and the call had nothing to return
Nothing special — it took roughly 83 us, continuing the same linear trend, since select() has no hard ceiling on descriptor count

It raised ValueError: filedescriptor out of range in select(). fd_set is a fixed-width bitmap of FD_SETSIZE bits (1024 on glibc), a compile-time constant that no ulimit can raise, so a descriptor numbered at or above it cannot be represented at all
explanation: select()'s linear cost comes from copying and scanning the whole bitmap on both sides of every call, but its fatal flaw is the bitmap's fixed width. Raising ulimit -n lets you open more files; it does not widen fd_set. That hard ceiling of roughly a thousand connections — not the microseconds — is why C10K required a new syscall, and why epoll and kqueue separate registration (which lives in the kernel) from waiting (which returns only the ready list).

question: 8,192 bytes are sitting in a socket's receive buffer. Your loop is edge-triggered (EPOLLET) and, on the readiness event, you perform exactly one recv() of 1,024 bytes and return to the wait. What happens next?
options: The next wait reports the descriptor again, because 7,168 bytes remain unread and readiness reflects the current buffer state

No further event ever arrives for that descriptor and the remaining 7,168 bytes are stranded. Edge-triggered reports a transition, not a state, so the connection hangs silently — the peer waits for a reply to a request that is sitting complete in your kernel buffer
The kernel discards the unread 7,168 bytes once the event is consumed, so the peer sees a truncated request and eventually times out
The descriptor is automatically deregistered from the epoll set and subsequent recv() calls fail with EBADF
explanation: Level-triggered reports 'this descriptor is readable right now' and so re-reports as long as any byte remains, which makes a partial read safe. Edge-triggered reports 'something arrived since I last told you' and is only re-armed once you have drained to EAGAIN and new data subsequently arrives. Option A is the tempting answer because it describes level-triggered behaviour, and it is exactly the assumption that makes this bug ship: under light load messages arrive small enough to be drained in one read, so tests pass and production hangs.

question: Your single-threaded event loop needs to serve a file from disk. Someone suggests just registering the file descriptor with epoll and reading it when it reports readable. Why does this not work?
options: epoll rejects regular files at epoll_ctl time with EPERM, so the registration fails immediately and loudly
It works correctly but is slower than a thread pool, because the kernel must check the page cache on every epoll_wait() call

A regular file is always reported ready. Readiness has no meaning for storage — there is no notion of 'not yet arrived' — so epoll says yes instantly and your subsequent read() parks the loop thread for the full disk latency, stalling every other connection. This is why libuv, Node and Netty hand file I/O to a thread pool, and why completion-based io_uring can do async disk I/O when epoll structurally cannot
It works, but only if the file is opened with O_NONBLOCK, which makes read() return EAGAIN until the page cache is warm
explanation: This is the sharpest illustration of readiness versus completion. Readiness answers 'will this operation block?', which is only a meaningful question for something that can be waiting on data from elsewhere. A disk file's bytes are always obtainable, just possibly slowly, so the answer is always yes and the information content is zero. Completion-based interfaces submit the whole operation and report when the bytes are already in your buffer, which is why io_uring — not epoll — is the answer for asynchronous file I/O.

question: In the Build It, 600 requests through the single-threaded selector server produced 2,993 partial writes and 1,200 fragmented reads. What does that tell you about how to write a non-blocking server?
options: Partial I/O is the common case, not an edge case: TCP is a byte stream that preserves order and bytes and nothing else, so every connection needs an input buffer with explicit framing (drained with a while, not an if) and an output queue holding whatever send() did not accept, with EVENT_WRITE registered only while that queue is non-empty
It shows the socket buffers were misconfigured; with default SO_SNDBUF and SO_RCVBUF sizes, send() and recv() transfer complete messages and neither buffer is necessary
It shows the selector was reporting spurious readiness events, which is a known level-triggered artifact that edge-triggered mode eliminates
It means the server was CPU-bound: partial writes only occur when the process cannot keep up with the kernel's drain rate
explanation: The blocking API hides both problems — sendall() loops internally, and you can simply call recv() again on your own call stack — so people meet them for the first time when they go single-threaded, and both loops must be turned inside out into state that survives across event-loop iterations. Larger buffers only make the failure rarer and therefore harder to find; a slow client, a congested link or a large payload brings it straight back. Registering EVENT_WRITE only while the output queue is non-empty matters too, since a connected socket is nearly always writable and a permanent write interest turns the loop into a 100%-CPU spin.
